data.title: Alerus expects 2026 net interest margin of ~3.55%-3.65% with mid-single-digit loan growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alerus Financial Corporation (ALRS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong first quarter to begin 2026" and "reported net income of $23 million or $0.89 per diluted share," with "return on average assets was 1.79%" and "return on average tangible common equity was approximately 22%" (President, CEO & Member of Board of Directors Katie Lorenson).
  • "Growth in highly valuable HSA balances sourced through our benefit services platform" left "nearly 1/4 of the deposits sourced from our integrated and synergistic business lines" and "more than 40% of our revenue are fee-based, capital-light and recurring" (President, CEO & Member of Board of Directors Lorenson).
  • "We recorded a reserve release of $4.9 million during the quarter, while maintaining an allowance for credit losses of 1.25% of total loans" and "repurchased $6 million of common stock" as "tangible book value per share increased to $18.15" (President, CEO & Member of Board of Directors Lorenson).
  • "We generated adjusted diluted EPS of $0.89" and "reported net interest margin expanded 8 basis points to 3.77%, a new post-IPO high" (Executive VP & CFO Alan Villalon).

Outlook

  • "Our 2026 guidance has improved" and the company "expect[s]" loans "to grow at a mid-single-digit rate for the full year despite more than $400 million of contractual maturities" and deposits "to grow in the low single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • "A net interest margin of approximately 3.55% to 3.65% for 2026" and "Full year return on assets will exceed 1.25%" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • "Adjusted noninterest income" is expected "to grow in the mid-single digits" and "Total net revenue growth in the mid-single digits with noninterest expense growth in the low single digits will continue to support positive operating leverage" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • "For each additional 25 basis point cut in rates, we would expect net interest margin to improve roughly 3 to 5 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).

Financial Results

  • "Net interest income remained stable at $44.9 million" and "core margin was 3.52%, representing a 35 basis point improvement from the core margin in the fourth quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • "Deposits increased 3.7% on a period-end basis" and "noninterest-bearing deposits increased 6.2% linked quarter and now represents approximately 19.7% of total deposits" while "our loan-to-deposit ratio improved to 92.8%" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • "Total revenue" in Retirement and Benefit Services "increased to $17.4 million" while "HSA deposits grew 7.1% to approximately $218 million" with "an average cost of roughly 10 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • "Asset quality improved meaningfully" though "net charge-offs were 71 basis points" driven by "a single $6.4 million charge-off"; separately, "nonperforming assets declined $15.4 million linked quarter" and "criticized loans were down 43% year-over-year" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).

Q&A

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked to "unpack the decline in plan participants in AUA" and why it was "revenue neutral"; President, CEO & Member of Board of Directors Lorenson said, "it was driven by the exit of a large lower-margin legacy relationship and replaced with a new partnership that has much higher levels of profitability, but lower levels of assets and participants," and Executive VP & Chief Retirement Services Officer Forrest Wilson added it was "a deliberate focus on us trying to achieve higher quality, more profitable business" and "largely a revenue-neutral event between the 2."
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked what supports confidence in "mid-single-digit growth" after a "softer start"; Executive VP and Chief Banking & Revenue Officer James Collins said, "We're staying the course," citing that the bank is "moving out some investor CRE" and that "our C&I pipelines are fairly robust in all markets, except for our ag."
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked about the "evolution" from reported margin to March exit margin; Executive VP & CFO Villalon said, "we do expect lower purchase accounting accretion on a go-forward basis" and "we're only anticipating 20 basis points of purchasing accounting accretion in the second quarter."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: pressed whether the NIM guide "exclude[s] accretion"; Executive VP & CFO Villalon replied, "No, that's full reported number" and said deposit seasonality is expected to matter because "in the second and third quarter, we typically see outflows of deposits, especially from our public funds."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked about capital deployment and buybacks; President, CEO & Member of Board of Directors Lorenson said priorities are "pretty consistent" and added, "We were active this quarter. We intend to remain active in our buyback going forward."
  • Damon Del Monte, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked about loan growth cadence; Executive VP and Chief Banking & Revenue Officer Collins said, "I would look to that, yes" on a flatter 2Q followed by improvement later in the year.
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked for MSR sizing and for more color on the largest nonaccrual; Executive VP & CFO Villalon said the MSR fair value was "a couple of hundred thousand," while Executive VP & COO Karin Taylor said on the nonaccrual, "We do continue to make progress currently negotiating a sale on that deal" and "we actually decreased our reserve from about 17% in Q1 to about 8% in Q2."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on the sustainability of margin and cadence of loan growth, including "what gives you confidence" (Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division) and "Just to be clear" about NIM mechanics (Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, using directive clarifications such as "No, that's full reported number" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon) and confidence statements like "We intend to remain active in our buyback going forward" (President, CEO & Member of Board of Directors Lorenson).
  • Versus Q4, the Q1 discussion placed more emphasis on moderating factors to NIM (purchase accounting accretion stepping down and public-funds seasonality), compared with Q4’s framing that "We do not have any further Fed cuts in our expectations for 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon in Q4 2025).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 reiterated the repositioning impact with details that "During 4Q, we sold $360 million of available-for-sale securities" and that restructuring lifted portfolio yield to "3.84%" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon), aligning with Q4’s description that sold securities had a "weighted yield of 1.7%" and reinvestment yield of "4.7%" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon in Q4 2025).
  • Guidance language tightened around NIM seasonality: Q1 warned "we typically see outflows of deposits, especially from our public funds" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon), while Q4 described NIM progression as "gradual" and "not really linear" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon in Q4 2025).
  • Credit narrative improved in Q1 with "nonperforming assets declined" and a "$4.9 million reserve release" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon), compared with Q4 noting that "Nonperforming loans ticked up higher" due to "an acquired purchase participation" and the allowance remaining at "1.53% of total loans" (President, CEO & Director Katie Lorenson in Q4 2025).

Risks and Concerns

  • "In the second and third quarter, we typically see outflows of deposits, especially from our public funds" and replacement funding can be higher cost, which management said "will put a little bit of pressure" on deposit costs (Executive VP & CFO Villalon).
  • Management flagged competitive deposit dynamics: "we are seeing competition pick up, especially on the deposit front" and "It's getting extremely competitive" (Executive VP & CFO Villalon; Executive VP and Chief Banking & Revenue Officer Collins).
  • Credit remained idiosyncratic but visible: "a single $6.4 million charge-off" drove net charge-offs (Executive VP & CFO Villalon), and management said it is "currently negotiating a sale" on the largest remaining nonaccrual (Executive VP & COO Taylor).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as confirmation of its repositioning, pointing to $23 million of net income, 3.77% reported NIM, deposit mix improvement, and a $4.9 million reserve release while maintaining a 1.25% ACL. For 2026, management guided to mid-single-digit loan growth, low-single-digit deposit growth, and ~3.55%-3.65% NIM, while acknowledging seasonal public-fund deposit outflows, step-down in purchase accounting accretion, and intensifying deposit competition as key swing factors as the year progresses.

data.title: ONEOK cut at Scotiabank as less compelling value proposition vs. other liquids names
data.description:
ONEOK (OKE) up 2.7% in Thursday's trading even as Scotiabank downgraded the oil and gas midstream operator to Sector Perform from Sector Outperform with an $89 price target, trimmed from $92, saying the shares are trading at a full valuation level largely a result of the broader midstream outperformance YTD even before the Iran war.

ONEOK's (OKE) earnings trajectory is largely known and understood, with most potential outperformance driven by producer development activity, Scotiabank's Brandon Bingham said, so "while the company is performing well at present, the relative value proposition is more compelling in other liquids names with higher Permian operational leverage and the same ability to capture regional pricing dislocations trading at similar or lower levels," such as Targa Resources (TRGP) and Kinetik Koldings (KNTK).

The analyst said he sees potential for sentiment to shift away from ONEOK (OKE) as "the realization sets in that the company does not have outsized earnings torque to higher commodity prices," and while peers are similarly well hedged, he sees the potential for "asymmetric downside" for the shares if a firm end comes to the Middle East conflict.

Earlier this week, ONEOK (OKE) reported Q1 earnings that missed estimates and raised guidance for FY 2026 adjusted EBITDA to a range of $8B-$8.5B.

data.title: VICI outlines 2026 AFFO of $2.44-$2.47 per share while raising full-year guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: VICI Properties (VICI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "VICI" is "in the business of sourcing, allocating and stewarding capital, invested accretively in experiential real estate of enduring value" (CEO & Director Edward Pitoniak).
  • "VICI had an active first quarter with approximately $1.2 billion in new capital commitments" and management said "the last 2 quarters... represent the first consecutive quarters during which VICI has announced more than $1 billion in new capital commitments sequentially" (President & COO John W. Payne).
  • "We announced an expansion of our long-term strategic relationship with Cain and Eldridge Industries by providing a $1.5 billion mezzanine loan" for One Beverly Hills, which "represents a $1.05 billion incremental commitment beyond our previously announced $450 million investment" and has "phased delivery... scheduled to commence in 2028" (President & COO Payne).
  • "VICI announced the pending $144 million acquisition of 4 real estate assets located in Alberta, Canada at an 8% cap rate" tied to Pure Casino Entertainment’s pending take-private acquisition of Gamehost (President & COO Payne).
  • "All gaming regulatory and shareholder approvals have been met for the previously announced $1.16 billion Golden transaction" and "we expect this acquisition to close today" (President & COO Payne).
  • "In the first quarter, on a year-over-year basis, we grew AFFO per share by 4.5% while only increasing our share count by roughly 1%" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer David Kieske).

Outlook

  • "We are raising AFFO guidance for 2026 in both absolute dollars as well as on a per share basis" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • "AFFO for the year ending December 31, 2026, is expected to be between $2.665 billion and $2.695 billion or between $2.44 and $2.47 per diluted common share" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • "Our guidance does not include the impact on operating results from any pending acquisitions without announced expected closing dates, possible future acquisitions or dispositions and related capital markets activity or other nonrecurring transactions or items" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).

Financial Results

  • "Our business generates about $650 million of free cash flow annually" and "VICI has an AFFO payout ratio of approximately 75%" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • "Our total debt is $17.1 billion" and "our net debt to annualized first quarter adjusted EBITDA is approximately 5x at the low end of our target leverage range of 5 to 5.5x" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • "We have a weighted average interest rate of 4.46%" and "a weighted average 5.7 years to maturity" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • "As of March 31, 2026, we have approximately $3.1 billion in total liquidity" including "approximately $480 million in cash and cash equivalents" and "$2.4 billion of availability under our revolving credit facility" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • "Subsequent to quarter end, we settled all remaining outstanding forward equity to partially fund the Golden transaction" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).

Q&A

  • Barry Jonas, Truist: "Your loan book is expanding again"—how to think about mix vs. sale-leaseback? Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske: "it's a strategic tool" and "we're at high single digits in terms of percent of total assets" and said loans can create "direct pathways to real estate ownership" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: status of the "Partner Property Growth Fund" incremental funding at Venetian? Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske: "It's still potentially happening" and noted the team "initially used $400 million of our capital"; President & COO Payne: "We're just not prepared to talk about that today" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske; President & COO Payne).
  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: why forward interest rate swaps? Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske: "These are forward starting interest rate swaps" to hedge "upcoming refis" with maturities "in September, December this year" and "February 2027" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup: tenant demand trends and Caesars regional lease performance/privatization press? President & COO Payne: regionals were "steady" and Las Vegas "turned the corner"; CEO & Director Pitoniak: "we do not comment on rumors" and said Caesars regional performance is showing "the benefits of the CapEx" (President & COO Payne; CEO & Director Pitoniak).
  • John DeCree, CBRE: sourcing "international financing sources" to lower cost of capital? Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske: "There could be an opportunity to issue debt" in Canada and management has "on and off looked at things overseas" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • Ravi Vaidya, Mizuho: Caesars regional lease coverage improvement and Century Casinos strategic review? CEO & Director Pitoniak: "we're not going to and never will comment" on tenant discussions; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske: Century has "announced strategic review" and "the asset-level coverage is very strong" (CEO & Director Pitoniak; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, with repeated probing on loans vs. sale-leasebacks, lease coverage, and tenant headlines, including "some press reports about Caesars" and questions on why swaps were executed (Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup; Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs).
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was positive and thematic, emphasizing durability and growth, including "we are strategically and organizationally committed to investing in experiential real estate" (CEO & Director Pitoniak).
  • In Q&A, management showed selective defensiveness on sensitive items, stating "we do not comment on rumors" and "we're not going to and never will comment on those kinds of discussions with any tenant" (CEO & Director Pitoniak).
  • Versus last quarter, the current call added more explicit boundary-setting around rumors and tenant negotiations, while maintaining a similar confidence posture around balance sheet capacity and external growth cadence (CEO & Director Pitoniak).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved up from "between $2.59 billion and $2.625 billion or between $2.42 and $2.45" to "between $2.665 billion and $2.695 billion or between $2.44 and $2.47" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).
  • Current-quarter deal discussion emphasized execution and closings, including "we expect this acquisition to close today" for Golden, whereas the prior quarter framed Golden as "expected later this year" (President & COO Payne; President & COO John W. Payne in prior-quarter remarks).
  • Current-quarter Q&A shifted from prior-quarter focus on a nonaccrual loan and lease-structure adjustments to a heavier focus on capital markets mechanics (forward swaps, alternative debt/equity pools) and tenant/rival market developments (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske; President & COO Payne).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged structural industry changes: "iGaming's expanding presence" and "the growing, though largely unregulated prediction markets" alongside "the stabilization of online sports betting" (President & COO Payne).
  • Tenant headline risk and negotiation sensitivity were evident as management stated "we do not comment on rumors" and declined to discuss tenant lease talks (CEO & Director Pitoniak).
  • Rate and refinancing preparation was highlighted through hedging ahead of "upcoming refis" and building "a portfolio of forward starting swaps" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Kieske).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as an acceleration in capital deployment, citing approximately $1.2 billion in new commitments, an expanded $1.5 billion One Beverly Hills mezzanine loan, and multiple gaming-related transactions including the $1.16 billion Golden deal expected to close the day of the call. The company raised 2026 AFFO guidance to $2.665-$2.695 billion ($2.44-$2.47 per share) while emphasizing its funding model of $650 million in annual free cash flow, approximately 75% payout ratio, and leverage near 5x, alongside increased focus on hedging ahead of near-term maturities and maintaining flexibility across loans, sale-leasebacks, and potential alternative capital sources.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street trades higher after a slew of economic data and earnings reports
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages traded higher on Thursday as investors assessed a slew of economic data, including the key PCE and GDP reports.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was +1.7%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +1%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.9%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, ten of the 11 S&P segments were in the green, with communication services leading the way higher. At the other end of the spectrum, info tech has been the worst performing area of the session. 

On the economic front, the employment cost index rose slightly more than expected in Q1. The core PCE inflation cooled as expected in March. Furthermore, initial jobless claims dropped to below 200K in the past week, far below consensus. The U.S. Q1 GDP rose less than expected in the initial print, while prices rose more. 

U.S. Treasury yields moved lower across the curve despite the longer end U.S. 30 Year Treasury yield (US30Y) topping 5%. The shorter end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) slipped 6 basis points to 3.88% and the U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 4 basis points to 4.39%. 

From an earnings perspective, Meta Platforms (META) beat on both the top and bottom lines in the first quarter. Amazon (AMZN) blew past AWS estimates while AI investments dented free cash flow. Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) reported strong financial growth and a quarterly beat. Microsoft (MSFT) reported fiscal third-quarter results that were above Wall Street’s forecast. 

Additionally, the Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting on Wednesday, citing uncertainty over the U.S. economic and global macro outlooks.

data.title: Kirby signals 2026 EPS up 5% to up 15% as inland pricing strengthens despite fuel and OEM timing headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kirby Corporation (KEX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Earlier today, we announced first quarter earnings per share of $1.50" and management tied the quarter to "improving market fundamentals in marine transportation" plus "continued strength in underlying demand for Power Generation" (CEO David W. Grzebinski).
  • Inland improved through the quarter, with utilization "averaged in the low 90% range" and "Spot pricing improved in the low single digits sequentially," while "Term contract renewals were flat to slightly up year-over-year" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • Coastal remained tight, with utilization "averaging in the mid-to-high 90% range" and "term contract renewal rates rising in the 20% range year-over-year" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • "We are increasing our EPS guidance range for the year to up 5% to up 15%, which is up from flat to up 12% previously" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • "In the first quarter of 2026, Marine Transportation segment revenues were $497.2 million and operating income was $89.7 million with an operating margin of 18%" (Executive VP & CFO Raj Kumar).
  • "Revenues for the first quarter of 2026 were $347 million with operating income of $23.3 million and an operating margin of 6.7%" for Distribution and Services, and management highlighted power generation order strength but noted "engine availability from OEMs is limiting how quickly some of that demand converts to revenue" (CFO Kumar).

Outlook

  • "We anticipate this timing issue could result in approximately $0.05 to $0.10 of earnings per share impact in the second quarter" due to fuel-cost lag in inland contracts (CEO Grzebinski).
  • "We expect approximately $0.10 to $0.15 of earnings per share impact in the second quarter" from delayed OEM engine deliveries shifting projects into the second half (CEO Grzebinski).
  • "Overall, we expect inland revenues to grow in the low to mid-single digits on a year-over-year basis with margins averaging in the high-teens to low 20% range for the full year" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • "We continue to expect mid-single-digit revenue growth year-over-year and operating margins in the high-teens" for Coastal Marine (CEO Grzebinski).
  • "Overall, the company expects segment revenues to be flat to slightly up for the full year with operating margins in the mid-to-high single digits" for Distribution and Services (CEO Grzebinski).

Financial Results

  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $97.7 million and capital expenditures were $48.3 million, resulting in free cash flow of $49.4 million" (CFO Kumar).
  • "As of quarter end, we had $58 million of cash with total debt of $983.4 million" and "available liquidity" of "$635.4 million" (CFO Kumar).
  • "In the first quarter of 2026, Kirby returned $52.7 million of capital to shareholders through share repurchase at an average price of $123.18" (CFO Kumar).
  • "We continue to execute on our focused and disciplined acquisition strategy by agreeing to acquire 23 barges and three high horsepower boats... for $95.8 million" (CFO Kumar).
  • "We continue to expect capital spending to range between $220 million and $260 million for the year" and "remain on track to generate cash flow from operations of $575 million to $675 million for the year" (CFO Kumar).

Q&A

  • Gregory Lewis, BTIG: Asked what is driving inland tightness; CEO Grzebinski said, "it's actually more volumes moving" and cited "more chemical activity" tied to disruptions abroad, adding, "We're seeing momentum actually build a little bit right now."
  • Gregory Lewis, BTIG: Asked about OEM engine visibility; CEO Grzebinski said, "We have good visibility through '27" and "most of them... are sold out to '29," while CFO Kumar added behind-the-meter prime systems are "probably looking at low double-digit margins" and service later is "probably going to be north of that."
  • Benjamin Mohr Mok, Citi: Asked why EPS guide rises while revenue/margin targets stay; CEO Grzebinski said guidance "just moved it up to the higher end of that range," and COO Christian O'Neil emphasized limited supply, noting "There were only 66 barges built last year" and "It's still $4.5 million to build a typical plain vanilla clean 30,000-barrel tank barge."
  • Adam Roszkowski, BofA: Asked term repricing cadence and renewal trend; CEO Grzebinski said "About 40% of the term portfolio reprices in the fourth quarter" and noted spot is "a good 10% above term pricing."
  • Adam Roszkowski, BofA: Asked Jones Act waiver risk; CEO Grzebinski said impacts are "almost nonexistent" near term, but "If waivers go beyond that... we could start to see some impact," and COO O'Neil warned it can create "a chilling effect" on mariner recruiting.
  • Scott Group, Wolfe: Asked spot-vs-term context and M&A; CEO Grzebinski said when markets get "sporty, it's more like 10% to 15%" and on acquisitions, "we remain very capital disciplined," adding that if Kirby cannot find deals, "you'll see us buy back our stock."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, repeatedly opening with congratulations (e.g., "Congrats on a good quarter" and "Congrats on the great results and also the raise") while pressing for durability of inland pricing, OEM timing visibility, and policy risk (Jones Act waiver).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and confident in demand/pacing, using phrases such as "very constructive" and "we feel really good" (CEO Grzebinski; COO O'Neil), while flagging specific near-term headwinds with quantified Q2 EPS impacts.
  • Versus the prior quarter’s focus on caution and a wider initial EPS range, management’s language in Q1 shifted more firmly toward momentum, while still emphasizing constraints and lags (fuel escalators and OEM availability).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved up: Q1 increased to "up 5% to up 15%" from Q4’s "flat to up 12%" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • Inland conditions strengthened: Q4 described utilization in the "mid- to high 80% range" with term renewals "down in the low single digits" (CEO Grzebinski; CFO Kumar), while Q1 cited utilization in the "low 90% range" and term renewals "flat to slightly up" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • Distribution and Services narrative kept the same core theme—power generation strength constrained by OEM supply—but Q1 added explicit Q2 EPS timing impact from delayed deliveries and highlighted behind-the-meter mix as a margin lever (CEO Grzebinski; CFO Kumar).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Global macro and geopolitical developments" were cited as creating "near-term variability" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • Inland faces fuel-cost timing risk: "cost escalators and rate recovery mechanisms... will lag the near-term fuel cost increases" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • Power generation faces execution timing constraints: "engine availability rather than end market demand continues to be the primary constraint" (CEO Grzebinski).
  • Policy uncertainty around Jones Act waivers was framed as limited near-term but potentially impactful if extended beyond contract durations (CEO Grzebinski).

Final Takeaway

Kirby management framed Q1 as an improving marine market with strengthening inland utilization and pricing, sustained coastal tightness, and power generation demand that remains strong but gated by OEM engine availability. The company raised full-year EPS growth guidance to up 5% to up 15% while quantifying two Q2 headwinds—fuel-cost recovery lag and delayed engine deliveries—alongside continued emphasis on disciplined capital allocation spanning share repurchases, tuck-in marine acquisitions, and maintained full-year cash flow and CapEx targets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Novocure outlines $690M-$710M 2026 revenue guidance as Optune Pax launch reaches 169 prescriptions in 7 weeks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NovoCure Limited (NVCR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “The leading news in the quarter was the FDA approval and subsequent U.S. launch of Optune Pax for patients with locally advanced pancreatic cancer.” (Executive Chairman William Doyle)

  • “In the 7 weeks, between the approval and quarter end, we certified 868 health care providers… Through March 31, we've received 169 prescriptions and completed 90 patient starts. We ended the quarter with 83 patients on therapy and a backlog of starts in the funnel.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)

  • “We are also pleased to report our first major payer coverage policy for Optune Pax with Elevance Health, covering over 30 million lives.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)

  • “Our global market segment also had an outstanding quarter with 17% active patient growth, driven by a promising launch in Spain.” (Chief Executive Officer Frank Leonard)

  • “The next major catalyst in our GBM program will be top line data from the Phase III TRIDENT trial expected in the second quarter.” (CEO Leonard)

  • “In March, we received national reimbursement in Japan and began treating commercial patients.” (CEO Leonard)

  • “We are exploring options now to modify the trial with the goals of compressing the time line to completion and significantly reducing the cost.” (CEO Leonard)

  • “Net revenue in the first quarter was $174 million, an increase of 12% year-over-year.” (Chief Financial Officer Christoph Brackmann)

Outlook

  • “Based on the strength of our first quarter results in GBM, we are updating our full year revenue guidance to a range of $690 million to $710 million, representing 5% to 8% growth.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “We are maintaining the range for combined revenue from Optune Lua and Optune Pax at $15 million to $25 million for the year.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “We are updating our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance this morning to a range from negative $15 million to breakeven.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “It will take a few quarters to fully understand the Optune Pax adoption curve and reimbursement dynamics.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)

Financial Results

  • “Net revenue in the first quarter was $174 million.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “Gross margin in the quarter was 78% compared to 75% in Q1 of 2025.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “Research and development costs in the quarter were $58 million… primarily driven by increased costs associated with the KEYNOTE D58 trial.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “G&A costs in the quarter were $86 million… we incurred a $43 million share-based compensation charge triggered by the approval of Optune Pax.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “Our net loss for the quarter was $71 million… Excluding the onetime share-based compensation expense, net loss was $28 million. Loss per share in the quarter was negative $0.62.” (CFO Brackmann)

  • “Our cash and investment balance as of March 31, 2026, was $432 million.” (CFO Brackmann)

Q&A

  • Jonathan Chang, Leerink Partners: “Can you provide any more color on the early Optune Pax launch… and help us contextualize how does this compare to the early lung cancer launch experience?” CEO Leonard: “We would say this is across whatever metric we want to look at, it is multiples of demand and really just a significantly different reception from the prescribing community.”

  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: asked about converting certified prescribers to active prescribers and whether early prescribers were predominantly trial investigators; CEO Leonard said, “the interest is much broader,” adding that “we've made significant investments over the last year into our HCP portal… and as a result, we've seen that speed to prescribe.”

  • Kevin DeGeeter, Ladenburg Thalmann: asked about payer funnel and CMS coverage timing; CEO Leonard said, “we expect around a year to 2 years to work through a coverage process in the U.S. for private payers and more on that full 2-year window to have a revision to the LCD for the Medicare coverage,” and added, “we have filed with the NCCN to request that guideline inclusion.”

  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked if RAS inhibitors are a headwind to Optune Pax; CEO Leonard said, “we are approved for locally advanced pancreatic cancer, which is a unique indication from where the current RAS inhibitor Phase III data is,” and Dr. Uri Weinberg added, “TTFields were found to inhibit c-Myc… [which] may actually complement the upstream KRAS inhibition.”

  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: asked about pent-up demand and OUS timing; CEO Leonard said, “we do think that reflects some pent-up demand,” while CFO Brackmann said, “we said second half for both Germany… as well as for Japan approval.”

  • Emily Bodnar, H.C. Wainwright: asked about prescription-to-start conversion timing; CEO Leonard said, “we saw typically about 2 weeks -- a little bit less than 2 weeks from a prescription to a start.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with repeated framing such as “significantly better than what we were thinking” (Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler) and multiple questions pressing for durability of the Optune Pax launch curve, reimbursement timing, and throughput from prescriptions to active patients.

  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing early launch indicators and timing caveats, including “it will take a few quarters to fully understand” (Executive Chairman Doyle) and “it's hard to give definitive answers around trends” (CEO Leonard).

  • Compared with Q4 2025, the tone shifted from launch preparation and first-year guidance framing to launch execution metrics and payer sequencing, while maintaining the profitability emphasis through updated adjusted EBITDA guidance (CFO Brackmann).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language moved from initial 2026 outlook in Q4 to an in-year update in Q1: “updating our full year revenue guidance to a range of $690 million to $710 million” (CFO Brackmann) vs. prior “annual net revenue guidance… $675 million to $705 million” (CFO Brackmann in Q4 2025).

  • Profitability framing tightened: “adjusted EBITDA guidance… negative $15 million to breakeven” (CFO Brackmann) vs. prior “negative $20 million to breakeven” (CFO Brackmann in Q4 2025).

  • Strategic focus broadened from “preparing to launch” Optune Pax and Optune Lua (CEO Leonard in Q4 2025) to reporting early Optune Pax funnel metrics and initial Optune Lua Japan reimbursement execution (Executive Chairman Doyle; CEO Leonard).

  • Analyst focus rotated from guidance construction and salesforce allocation (Q4) to launch trajectory, conversion timing, and reimbursement/LCD timelines (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • Reimbursement timing risk for Optune Pax remained central: “It will take a few quarters to fully understand the Optune Pax adoption curve and reimbursement dynamics.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)

  • Coverage sequencing was reiterated as a gating factor: “we expect around a year to 2 years… for private payers and more on that full 2-year window… for the Medicare coverage.” (CEO Leonard)

  • Clinical execution and cost control around large trials were highlighted: “this is a 700-plus patient trial, which we expect to fully enroll by the end of this year.” (CFO Brackmann)

Final Takeaway

NovoCure’s management characterized Q1 2026 as a “strong start to the year” with Optune Pax’s early U.S. launch producing 868 certified providers, 169 prescriptions, and 90 starts within seven weeks of approval, alongside initial payer progress via Elevance Health coverage. Management raised full-year revenue guidance to $690 million-$710 million and updated adjusted EBITDA guidance to negative $15 million to breakeven, while repeatedly flagging that reimbursement dynamics and broader coverage policies for Optune Pax are expected to take multiple quarters (and up to “a year to 2 years” for commercial payers and longer for Medicare LCD updates) to mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Google launches Gemini AI on Google-equipped vehicles
data.description:
• Vehicles equipped with Google Assistant will be getting an upgrade with Google’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Gemini AI Assistant, enabling drivers to ask for directions, get updates on their journey, and select radio stations or playlist options in a conversational tone, rather than using rigid voice commands.
  • The Gemini upgrade will be available not only to new Google-equipped vehicles but also to older vehicles through a software update. 
  • The rollout will begin with English in the U.S. and continue over the coming months.
  • Eligible users who are signed into their Google Account in their vehicle will see the option to upgrade to Gemini.
  • Once upgraded, Gemini can be accessed by either saying “Hey Google,” selecting the microphone icon on the home screen, or using the steering wheel controls.
  • On Wednesday, General Motors (GM)  announced that Gemini will be available in 4M vehicles from model year 2022.

data.title: Driven Brands rallies after receiving a PE buyout offer
data.description:
Activist hedge fund ADW Capital is offering $18 per share to buy Driven Brands (DRVN) in a deal that would value it at close to $3B, according to The Wall Street Journal. The move is part of ADW’s effort to turn around the company and remove private equity firm Roark Capital, which is Driven Brands (DRVN) majority owner. 

ADW Capital already holds a 3.7% stake in Driven Brands (DRVN). The firm wants the Driven Brands (DRVN) board to engage with it no later than May 15 and reserves the right to take its offer directly to shareholders.

Weighing in on a potential transaction, BTIG analyst Marvin Fong said that one challenge is that Driven Brands (DRVN) is already significantly leveraged, and it's unclear what mix of equity and debt will be used to finance the transaction. "DRVN is already carrying 3-3.5x net debt/EBITDA, and interest rates are elevated," he warned. However, Fong and his team continue to view Driven Brands' (DRVN) assets as undervalued and think the buyout bid could reduce the market discount to fair value

Shares of Driven Brands (DRVN) rallied 8.3% in afternoon trading to $13.80.

Driven Brands (DRVN) owns a portfolio of automotive service brands, including Take 5 Oil Change, Meineke, Maaco, CARSTAR, 1-800-Radiator & A/C, Fix Auto USA, ABRA, Uniban, VitroPlus, UniglassPlus, Docteur du Pare-Brise, Go! Glass and Auto Glass Now.

data.title: Balchem signals continued year-over-year growth amid petrochemical inflation risk
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Balchem Corporation (BCPC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We were extremely pleased with the financial results for the first quarter of 2026" and management said the quarter included "record first quarter consolidated sales, adjusted EBITDA, adjusted net earnings and adjusted EPS" while marking "the 27th consecutive quarter of quarterly year-over-year growth in adjusted EBITDA for Balchem" (CEO, President & Chairman Theodore Harris).
  • Management framed the demand backdrop as stable, saying, "We continue to see healthy demand across the vast majority of our end markets" while highlighting Human Nutrition & Health as "driven by healthy demand for both our unique portfolio of minerals, nutrients and vitamins and our food ingredients and solutions" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • Management emphasized near-term cost pressure, stating, "We are once again entering a period of significant inflation, largely petrochemical-based" and warned, "We are likely to experience some modest margin compression" due to the lag between inflation and pricing (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • "Our record first quarter net sales of $271 million were 8.1% higher than prior year" and "our gross margin percent expanded to 37.3% of sales" while "consolidated operating expenses for the first quarter were $45 million" (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Carl Bengtsson).
  • On VitaCholine, management highlighted a new publication: "The results showed that VitaCholine intake significantly enhanced functional connectivity within the working memory network, improving brain efficiency within 3 hours of consumption" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal numeric EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks or Q&A; instead, it said, "we do expect to deliver continued quarterly year-over-year growth on a consolidated basis over the coming quarters" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • On margins and inflation pass-through, management said, "We will once again leverage our robust global supply chain, our procurement expertise and our strong market positions to raise prices where necessary" while also reiterating, "we are likely to experience some modest margin compression" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s emphasis on tariffs and trade volatility, management focus shifted to the Middle East conflict, stating it could drive "potential supply chain disruptions" and higher input costs (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).

Financial Results

  • Revenue of $271 million vs. $266,504,330 (analysts estimates); adjusted EPS of $1.33 vs. $1.33 (analysts estimates).
  • "We reported record quarterly consolidated revenue of $271 million" and "consolidated net income closed the quarter at $40 million" with "diluted net earnings per share of $1.25 on a GAAP basis" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA was a record $74 million" and "cash flows from operations were $40 million with free cash flow of $34 million" while "our net debt was $96 million" and "we closed out the quarter with $73 million of cash on the balance sheet" (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson).
  • By segment, "Human Nutrition & Health segment saw sales of $172 million"; "Animal Nutrition & Health segment delivered sales of $62 million"; and "Specialty Products segment delivered quarterly sales of $35 million" (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson).

Q&A

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: "what percent of sales are branded"? CEO, President & Chairman Harris: "Our branded ingredients... make up about... 40% to 50% of our Human Nutrition & Health business today" and added that mix should increase because "that part of the business is obviously growing faster."
  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about extending VitaCholine beyond supplements and whether the Jets partnership can drive more beverage growth. CEO, President & Chairman Harris: "we've had pretty significant success more recently relative to sports beverages, energy drinks" and said "that nutritional beverage market is a significant opportunity for us and will grow rapidly over time" while marketing expands beyond the NFL to "the Bayern Munich Women's team" and "influencer areas, digital media."
  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked what drove gross margin strength and how to think about margins with inflation. Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson: cited mix where "our higher-margin businesses are the ones growing the fastest" and cautioned that with inflation, "we may see a little bit of a margin rate compression."
  • Raghuram Selvaraju, H.C. Wainwright & Co, LLC: asked about VitaCholine positioning for older adults and industrial demand sensitivity to energy activity; also asked about tax rate. CEO, President & Chairman Harris: the industrial business is "still not a measurable contributor" but "sales are up, demand is up"; on VitaCholine, the new study "could be an absolute breakthrough" opening a "huge adult cognition market" though "we need more studies." Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson: "we tend to use 23% effective tax rate" and said it may be "higher in Q2" and "lower in the back half of the year."
  • Daniel Harriman, Sidoti & Company, LLC: asked for an international growth update and European monogastric recovery. CEO, President & Chairman Harris: international expansion is "a primary strategic focus" with "higher growth rates in most international locations versus the U.S." Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson: Europe showed "a double-digit volume improvement" post-antidumping, but the Middle East conflict may affect end markets via "higher input costs."
  • Artem Chubarov, Rothschild & Co Redburn: asked about H&H subcategory growth and Specialty Products profitability by region. CEO, President & Chairman Harris: minerals and nutrients saw "double-digits growth" while Food Ingredients and Solutions grew "lower to mid-single digits"; Performance Gases demand was healthy "both in the U.S. and in Europe" and Plant Nutrition had "significant margin improvement" despite no Q1 growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, repeatedly congratulating management while pressing on mix sustainability, inflation/margins, and VitaCholine monetization (e.g., "the 37.2% surprised on the upside" and "how should we think about gross margins going forward?" — Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A on growth and portfolio strength, while acknowledging inflation headwinds using qualified language such as "likely" and "modest" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris; Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s tariff-focused uncertainty, management’s tone remained confident but shifted toward cost inflation and supply chain disruption risk tied to the Middle East conflict (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter messaging emphasized petrochemical inflation and the Middle East conflict ("potential supply chain disruptions"), while the previous quarter centered on tariffs and trade volatility and the company’s intra-regional manufacturing model (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • Investor discussion remained consistent around branded-ingredient marketing (Jets and Bayern Munich) and clinical study pipeline; Q1 added a newly published VitaCholine cognitive-connectivity study and explicitly discussed repositioning marketing toward aging adults (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • Operationally, Europe monogastric improvement remained a recurring topic; Q1 added that volume improvement was "double-digit" and paired it with monitoring new geopolitical cost impacts (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "significant inflation, largely petrochemical-based" and warned about "modest margin compression" due to timing of price actions (CEO, President & Chairman Harris).
  • Management flagged "potential supply chain disruptions" from the Middle East conflict and said higher raw materials and freight "will either be offset or passed on to our customers" (CEO, President & Chairman Harris; Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson).
  • On tax rate variability, management said quarterly rates may move above 23% before trending back, due to "timing" and "changes in state tax laws" (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer and GM of Animal Nutrition & Health Bengtsson).

Final Takeaway

Balchem management described Q1 2026 as a record quarter across sales and key profit measures, with broad segment growth and continued investment behind branded nutrients—particularly VitaCholine—while cautioning that petrochemical-driven inflation and Middle East-related supply chain risks could create modest margin-rate pressure as pricing actions catch up.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CNH reaffirms 2026 adjusted EPS of $0.35 to $0.45 as it targets $500M dealer inventory reduction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CNH Industrial N.V. (CNH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "First quarter results were as expected and guided." (CEO, Head of Agriculture & Executive Director Gerrit Marx) "We are now passing through what we expect to be the lowest period of the current ag industry cycle" and "we also expect Q1 2026 to be the lowest quarter of the year." (CEO Marx)

  • "We kept production levels low in order to manage and contain channel inventory." (CEO Marx) "Ag dealer inventory levels remained unchanged since the beginning of the year by design" and "the flat levels are in line with our overall plan to have the dealers reduce their inventories by about $500 million this year." (CEO Marx)

  • "Consolidated revenues were $3.8 billion" and "Industrial adjusted EBIT was a loss of $45 million." (CEO Marx) "For the quarter, adjusted net income was $21 million, with adjusted EPS at $0.01." (CEO Marx)

  • "Free cash flow from Industrial Activities was a $569 million outflow." (CEO Marx) "Agriculture Q1 net sales were about $2.6 billion" and "Gross margin was 19.1% from 20% a year ago." (CFO and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer James A. Nickolas)

  • "We have recently entered into a strategic relationship with Abilene Machine through a minority equity stake." (CEO Marx) "The relationship will allow CNH to offer our dealer network a comprehensive aftermarket parts portfolio with upcoming access to the Abilene Machine portfolio of all makes parts." (CEO Marx)

Outlook

  • "We are comfortable reaffirming our net sales guidance of flat to down 5%." (CFO Nickolas) "We are reaffirming our EBIT margin guidance of 4.5% to 5.5%." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "In construction... we still forecast our own net sales to be about flat year-over-year" and "EBIT margin is still forecasted to be between 1% and 2%." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "We reaffirm our forecast for 2026 industrial net sales to be flat to down 4% year-over-year and industrial EBIT margin to be between 2.5% and 3.5%." (CFO Nickolas) "Industrial free cash flow is still forecasted to be between $150 million and $350 million" and "Adjusted EPS is reaffirmed at between $0.35 and $0.45." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "Our order books are full for the second quarter" and "we're expecting agriculture net sales to be about flat on a year-over-year basis." (CEO Marx) "Construction net sales will be higher in the mid-teens" and "Financial Services net income in Q2 will be lower year-over-year by $20 million to $25 million." (CFO Nickolas)

Financial results

  • "Agriculture adjusted EBIT margin was 1% from 5.4% in Q1 2025." (CFO Nickolas) "The positive pricing and the cost saving contribution only partially offset negative regional mix and tariff impacts." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "Construction net sales in the quarter were lower 3% year-over-year to $574 million" and "Q1 adjusted EBIT margin was negative 4.9%." (CFO Nickolas) "We held back sales while working out a supplier quality issue to protect the customer. That issue is now resolved, and those sales will be made up in Q2." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "In Financial Services, segment net income in the quarter was $74 million" and "Retail originations in the first quarter were $2.2 billion, and the managed portfolio ended the quarter at $28 billion." (CFO Nickolas) "Sequential delinquency rates increased slightly to 3.5%, primarily driven by persistent economic difficulties in South America." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "During the first 3 months of 2026, we repurchased $26 million worth of CNH stock at an average price of about $10.70 per share." (CFO Nickolas)

Q&A

  • Timothy Thein, Raymond James: "can you maybe give some context" on build rates and regional orders? CEO Marx: "we are fully booked in Q2, and we have a pretty healthy coverage already for Q3" and "the region where we have extra efforts and extra attention is... Latin America, particularly Brazil."

  • Esther Osinaiya, Morgan Stanley: "can you just give us a little bit more color" on tariff impact and offsets? CFO Nickolas: "the ag business, really no net change versus prior guidance" and "For construction... now we expect about a 600 basis point impact on our construction margins compared to... roughly 500 points."

  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer: "how you're thinking about back half scenarios" given fertilizer and transportation costs? CFO Nickolas: "if they were to persist throughout the year, the gross net increase in cost could be up around $70 million" and "we would take action at some point if this elevated cost environment persists."

  • Jamie Cook, Truist: setup for 2027 and company self-help? CEO Marx: "2027 will be probably a better year than 2026." CFO Nickolas: "we're also underproducing versus retail in 2026... by about 4%."

  • Kyle Menges, Citigroup: competition, pricing, inventories, and tariff mechanics? CEO Marx: "we do see a continued positive price cost development for us" and "we... alluded to a $500 million further reduction of our global channel inventory." CFO Nickolas: "it kind of depends on the HTS codes."

  • David Raso, Evercore ISI: construction strategy and underproduction mix? CEO Marx: "we have restarted our discussions with several partners for our construction business" and "over the course of the remainder of 2026 or first half of 2027, we should be clearer." CFO Nickolas: "more underproduction happening in combines" and "probably a little bit more under production occurring in Brazil."

  • Evan McCaul, BMO: credit dynamics and reserves? CFO Nickolas: "The real increases are Brazil, primarily and secondarily, Argentina" and "I would expect an uptick in delinquencies in Q2 of this year, like we always see."

  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: South America duration? CEO Marx: "this could drag into '27 as well" and "replacement will provide a floor."

  • Daniela Costa, Goldman Sachs: construction price/cost and Q2 breakeven? CFO Nickolas: "for the full year, we are not forecasting positive net pricing on the construction business" and "we do believe it will be above breakeven in Q2."

  • Edward Jackson, Northland: farm bill and E15? CEO Marx: "It is not going to suddenly boost... major equipment demands" and "making E15 fuel... permanent... has quite some positive impact on corn."

  • Judah Aronovitz, UBS: quarterly price/cost and pass-through confidence? CFO Nickolas: "price cost for ag per quarter, yes, that just remained positive" and "On the construction business, less confident."

Sentiment analysis

  • "given the crosswinds" and "how you're thinking about back half scenarios" reflected a slightly negative to neutral analyst tone focused on cost inflation and macro uncertainty. (Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer)

  • "it's a much more competitive fragmented market" and "less confident" reflected management acknowledging pressure points in construction pricing power. (CFO Nickolas)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s emphasis that "2026 should represent the trough of the cycle" (CFO Nickolas, Q4 2025), management’s Q1 framing reiterated caution while stating "we remain more committed than ever to strengthening the company" and "we are optimistic" on select demand supports. (CEO Marx)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language was consistent: Q1 reiterated "Adjusted EPS is reaffirmed at between $0.35 and $0.45" (CFO Nickolas) versus Q4: "Adjusted EPS is forecasted to be between $0.35 and $0.45" (CFO Nickolas).

  • Tariff framing tightened on construction: Q1 said "now we expect about a 600 basis point impact on our construction margins" (CFO Nickolas) versus Q4: "tariffs... now estimated to have a gross impact of around 500 basis points of margin." (CFO Nickolas)

  • Construction strategy continued on the same timeline: Q1 said "over the course of the remainder of 2026 or first half of 2027, we should be clearer" (CEO Marx) versus Q4: "When there is news to share, we will include those in our earnings calls in 2026 or 2027." (CEO Marx)

Risks and concerns

  • "Market risk in South America is elevated due to tighter credit and delays in government-backed financing in Brazil." (CFO Nickolas) "we... have tightened underwriting standards." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "Section 301 investigations are ongoing... We have not included any factors for that in this forecast." (CFO Nickolas) "Transportation costs and the tariff payments are another watch point." (CFO Nickolas)

  • "Selling a machine is one thing, collecting its monthly installments is another." (CEO Marx) "we have been thoughtfully managing that jointly with our network partners during this difficult period." (CEO Marx)

Final takeaway

Management said Q1 tracked expectations with low production to control dealer inventory, reaffirmed full-year guidance including adjusted EPS of $0.35 to $0.45 and industrial free cash flow of $150 million to $350 million, and highlighted tariff mechanics as net neutral to 2026 agriculture guidance but a higher construction margin headwind. Management also pointed to Brazil credit stress and rising logistics costs as key variables, while emphasizing dealer network actions, product launches, and a minority stake in Abilene Machine to broaden aftermarket parts access.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
